title:
Clinical Scanning
Workflow
subtitle: Redesigning the clinical scanning workflow for speed, clarity, and decision-making in real treatment conditions.

slides.title:
Clinical Scanning
Workflow
slides.description:
The patient is in the chair. The scanner is ready. The system isn't.
iTero is a digital intraoral scanner used chairside during live patient appointments. I redesigned Align Technology's RX, Scan, and View workflow into one continuous system.

slides.label: The System
slides.title: A system used during live patient treatment
slides.content: iTero is used chair-side. Three phases — RX, Scan, View — feed into each other. A problem in one disrupts the whole appointment.
issues: RX → define patient, procedure, case details
Scan → capture a real-time 3D model
View → validate before lab submission
slides.highlight: Every decision happens under time pressure — with the patient in the chair.

slides.label: The Problem
slides.title: The Rx was built as a sequential form
slides.content: Sequential, popup-heavy, and rigid — it forced clinical decisions before clinicians had the information to make them.
issues: Every tooth, material, and spec triggered a modal
No multi-select — procedures set tooth by tooth
Changing a procedure meant starting over
Setup had to finish before scanning could begin
slides.highlight: At 37 million cases a year, even one unnecessary popup per case is not a small problem.

slides.label: Who It Affected
slides.title: The problem reached further than the chair
slides.description: Setup failures didn't stop at the clinician. Every incomplete field and blocking modal sent ripple effects downstream.

issues.title: Dentists
issues.description: Every extra click was time not spent on the patient — setup friction happened chair-side, with the patient watching.

issues.title: Clinical staff
issues.description: Incomplete cases and mandatory-field errors generated resubmissions — administrative work created by avoidable UI gaps.

issues.title: Labs
issues.description: Received Rx data that was incomplete or unstructured for complex procedures like All-on-X and multi-phase treatments.

slides.label: Why Now
slides.title: Three signals converged at once
slides.content: 3Shape, Medit, and Shining3D had already modernised their Rx flows. The iTero architecture had been patched too many times to patch again — the only path forward was a rebuild.
issues: Competitors had modernised — iTero's Rx flow was visibly behind
At this scale, every extra popup per case was expensive
The codebase couldn't absorb another patch — only a rebuild would hold
slides.highlight: At 37 million cases a year, one unnecessary popup per case adds up fast.

slides.label: Research & Discovery
slides.title: What I heard in the room
slides.content: I interviewed doctors across procedure types — restorative specialists, general dentists, orthodontists. I asked each one to walk me through a real case: where they slowed down, what they wished worked differently. Five themes came back in every session.

quotes.text: It feels like a long boring form — and I'm doing it with a patient in the chair.
quotes.author: Restorative Specialist

quotes.text: I change the procedure multiple times before I even begin scanning.
quotes.author: General Dentist

quotes.text: There's always another popup. I just want to get to the scan.
quotes.author: Orthodontist

quotes.text: Every time I select a tooth, something opens. I can't see what I've already done.
quotes.author: Restorative Specialist

quotes.text: The moment I go looking for a tool, I lose my rhythm completely.
quotes.author: General Dentist

slides.label: Design Goals
slides.title: Six problems to solve. Six ways to measure them.

goals.title: Flow alongside the scan
goals.description: Setup should not gate the capture — Rx runs parallel, not before.

goals.title: Eliminate one-at-a-time configuration
goals.description: Multi-select teeth, group materials, apply in one step.

goals.title: Editable at any point
goals.description: Change procedure or material mid-workflow without restarting.

goals.title: Keep scanning uninterrupted
goals.description: Tools accessible without leaving the capture context.

goals.title: One visual language
goals.description: No icon memorisation, no mixed styles across tools.

goals.title: A clear completion signal
goals.description: One done state in View — re-checking becomes a choice, not a reflex.

kpis: Time from case open to first scan action
Tool interactions that exit the scan context

slides.label: Constraints
slides.title: What the design had to work across

issues.title: Three surfaces
issues.description: Scanner, MIDC desktop, and web — gloved hands, no hover.

issues.title: Backward compatibility
issues.description: Old Rx stayed live for in-progress cases.

issues.title: Feature-flagged
issues.description: On or off per company — learnable on first contact, every time.

issues.title: Read and demo states
issues.description: Three states designed in from day one — not retrofitted.

issues.title: Labs out of scope
issues.description: Not in MVP — architecture built to absorb them later.

description: These weren't background conditions — each one directly shaped a design decision. Ignoring any of them would have meant shipping something that worked in a prototype and broke in the clinic.

slides.label: User Flow Mapping
slides.title: Mapping the flow before touching the design
slides.content: With the goals in place, I mapped the full appointment as clinicians actually moved through it — not the designed flow, the real one. Three breakpoints emerged immediately.
issues: RX setup blocked the clinician before any scan began
Tool access broke the clinician's rhythm mid-scan
No completion signal — View felt unfinished
slides.highlight: The friction wasn't random — it lived at every phase handoff.

slides.label: Interactive Prototype
slides.title: A coded prototype to test decisions before development
slides.content: Figma shows what a toolbar looks like — it can't show whether placement breaks scanning rhythm the moment a clinician reaches for a tool. I built the prototype in code before any screens so timing failures surfaced immediately.
bullets2: Timing failures caught before any screen was locked
Tool-reach problems visible in motion, not static frames
A decision made in the morning, tested the same afternoon
slides.highlight: The toolbar placement decision could not have been made correctly from static screens.

slides.title: RX
slides.subtitle: Defining the patient, procedure, and case before scanning — and the setup gate I had to clear first.

slides.label: RX Setup — Before
slides.title: Setup blocked the scan before it could start
slides.content: The old product was a dense, multi-step form — patient and procedure gated behind a blocking modal before scanning could begin.
issues: Patient selection required a multi-step modal
Procedure had to be defined upfront
Dropdown disconnected from the dental chart
slides.highlight: Every second here was a second the patient sat waiting.
slides.caption: Old RX setup — the blocking multi-step form. (Add img-uykask3eo.webp)

slides.label: RX Setup — Exploration
slides.title: Three approaches to RX setup
slides.dir1Desc: Broke setup into a step-by-step wizard — organised, but more clicks and a slower start.
slides.dir2Desc: A collapse/expand layout — tap to open, read, close, repeat. In practice it meant constantly reopening the same section. That friction alone killed it.
slides.dir3Desc: Everything visible at once — no expanding, no steps. The full setup readable in a single view.

slides.label: RX Setup — After
slides.title: Users start scanning right away
slides.content: Users start right away — patient context fills in as they go, not as a prerequisite.
issues: Inline patient selection — no modal
Patient context stays visible throughout
Procedure selection tied to teeth structure
slides.caption: New RX setup — single-view, no blocking form. (Add vid-aouv40ns6o0.mp4)

slides.label: RX Tooth Selection
slides.title: Selecting teeth should be as fast as pointing at them
slides.beforeDescription: Every tooth needed its own modal — click, confirm, close, repeat. No overview of what you'd already done.
slides.afterDescription: Teeth selected directly on the chart — one or many at once — with procedures assigned inline.
beforeBullets: One blocking modal per tooth — no multi-select
afterBullets: Select one or many teeth in one interaction
Color-coded procedures visible on the chart
Full treatment plan visible without opening anything

slides.title: Scan
slides.subtitle: Capturing a 3D model before lab submission — starting with where the tools should live.

slides.label: Scan Toolbar — Before
slides.title: Tools scattered, with no home
slides.content: Icons built up from different eras, different styles, no shared visual language — and no consistent place to find them mid-scan.
issues: No shared visual language across icons
No dedicated place for tools
Every new feature added more scatter
slides.caption: Old toolbar — scattered, mixed-style icons. (Add img-c1djiu0kh0.webp — DESIGNER: confirm this image shows icon fragmentation, else supply a real old-toolbar screenshot.)

slides.label: Toolbar — Exploration
slides.title: I tested three placements with clinicians during live scanning
slides.dir1Desc: Bottom center — sat across the occlusal area, obscuring the scan detail that needed the most visual attention during capture.
slides.dir2Desc: Vertical right rail — consistent location, but always-visible consumed viewport space even when no tool was needed.
slides.dir3Desc: Top-right corner, collapsible — the only position clinicians could reach without pulling their eyes off the model. With placement settled, the icon problem surfaced: a correctly placed toolbar with unmemorable icons was still broken.

slides.label: Scan Toolbar — After
slides.title: One toolbar, one visual language
slides.content: A collapsible toolbar anchored to the top-right, rebuilt with a unified icon system — consistent line weight, consistent style, labeled.
issues: Toolbar fixed to one corner — always findable, never in the way
Unified icon set — each tool reads at a glance
New tools slot in without requiring new conventions
bullets2: Toolbar and procedure icons rebuilt as one visual system
Consistent line weight and style across every tool
slides.caption: New collapsible toolbar + unified icon set. (Add vid-g2r58c0dp9s.mp4)

slides.label: Multi-scan & Compare
slides.title: Managing multiple scans and comparing them
slides.content: Multi-visit cases need more than one scan. Clinicians had to track changes between appointments. Pre- and post-treatment scans sit in tabs, with overlay and adjustable opacity.
bullets2: Add scans within one session — tabs keep them organised
Overlay scans with adjustable opacity — compare in real time
What clinicians held in memory, now visible on screen
slides.highlight: Comparing two scans became visual instead of mental.

slides.label: View Tools
slides.title: From icon bars to clinical decision panels
slides.beforeDescription: Unlabelled icon clusters with no hierarchy — every meaning memorised, nothing self-explanatory.
slides.afterDescription: Named panels with one clear primary action — the same structure across every tool, nothing to memorise.
slides.highlight: Before the redesign, clinicians paused to recall what each icon meant. Named panels removed that step.

slides.label: Design System
slides.title: Every component built in three states from the start
slides.content: Every pattern from the redesign became a reusable component, built in three states from the start: editable, read-only, and demo. Retrofitting read mode after the fact produces visual inconsistency — in a clinical context, that inconsistency erodes trust.
issues: Input states — default, filled, disabled, error
Button states — default, hover, pressed, disabled
Selection controls — checkbox, indeterminate, disabled
Progress states — uploading, processing, complete, failed
slides.highlight: The three-state architecture became the shared language the rest of the product was built on.
slides.caption: Design system components formalised from the redesign

outcomes.title: No blocking gate to the scan
outcomes.description: Case open → first scan with no mandatory setup step. [ADD: setup time measurement from usability testing or post-launch analytics]

outcomes.title: Multi-tooth selection in one step
outcomes.description: Select a range, apply material and spec in one interaction — no tooth-by-tooth modals. [ADD: multi-tooth task completion rate from testing]

outcomes.title: Zero blocking modals on the critical path
outcomes.description: Between case open and scan start, no popup blocks progress.

outcomes.title: Mid-workflow changes without restarts
outcomes.description: Procedure and materials changeable at any point. [ADD: restart rate before/after from testing or analytics]

slides.label: Reflection
whatWorked: A coded prototype surfaced timing failures static screens miss — the toolbar decision depended on it.
Mapping the flow first made the three-phase structure obvious before any design began.
whatFailed: Toolbar exploration ran sequentially, not constraints-first — the rejections were obvious from sketch one.
Grouped materials definition came in late, compressing testing time for edge cases.
whatYoudDoDifferently: Define toolbar placement constraints before generating options.
Bring grouped materials into scope in week one — the highest-leverage feature, found too late.
slides.whatYouCouldntMeasure: Outcomes were validated in prototype testing, not the field. [ADD: post-launch resubmission rate, setup time, and mandatory-field error rate.]
slides.whatYouLearned: The Rx looked like a form problem. It was an architecture problem — setup gated the scan instead of running alongside it. Getting that diagnosis right before Figma shaped everything.

slides.title: Thank You
slides.subtitle: Three phases that were built separately now work as one appointment.
